The cafeteria at PS/IS 208 school in Glen Oaks, Queens, racked up 18 violations for problems including rodents, flies, and not having a hand-washing station. This educational facility was among those that accumulated the most violations since 2020.

The ranking of schools with the most violations also includes PS 138, Crown Heights, Brooklyn, which was cited 16 times.

Also in Brooklyn, PS K315 school racked up 15 violations in the last three years.

On the other hand, PS 131 in Borough Park, Brooklyn also had 15 violations, for cockroaches, flies and mice, among others. Several of these were reported in March 2023.

Every NYC family can search for their children school cafetería and/or kitchen in this database that shows current inspection data (from 2020 onward) from the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

About the data

The information about inspections of NYC school cafeterias and kitchens has several problems. First, there is more than one database. One has records from December 18, 2016 through December 12, 2019, although the website states that the data was last updated on August 23, 2021. This dataset has 24.6K rows and 22 columns. The other, contains inspections from October 27, 2020, to March 23, 2023. The records have almost 11.1K rows and 25 columns. I do not understand why it was not all kept in the same database. In both cases, the data are available on the NYC Open Data website. The website does not provide more information. For this article we decided to work with the most recent information: from 2020 onwards.

When we started to analyze the data we encountered several drawbacks. 1.38 percent of the records were not completed with latitude and longitude. Many rows have the name of the school misspelled. There are numerous addresses that do not match the educational institution listed. Almost 2 percent of the total schools have the coordinates (latitude and longitude) incorrect. About 3 percent of the rows are missing most of the data. Some of the school names are misspelled. This is just to mention the most important ones.

Accordingly, it was very difficult to analyze the information. After eight days of work, we were able to correct some of these errors. Unfortunately, these problems made the production of this article difficult. Although we analyzed and, of course, checked every value and number in this publication, but if its an error, it was involuntary and was a consecuense of the data published.

This article was written by Federico D. Teijeiro. For further information on this article, please visit my repository on GitHub, where you can find the data used. For more information about the author, his work and publications, please click here and/or here.
